So when I started teaching college, it wasn't after years of easing into it. It was just before the pandemic and within months we were thrown entirely online. A few years later, as classrooms were settling back into rhythm, generative AI arrived Another jolt. So two major shifts happen in a short span, both forcing me to rethink the mechanics of teaching and learning. I'm George Lanya at Santa Clara University. I teach genetics and human physiology among other courses. And at Foothill College I teach the full anatomy physiology sequence. Teaching is really my third career. I started in biotech, then moved into academic research and only later into the classroom. That background means I don't see myself as someone guarding traditions. I approach teaching the way I approach the experiments, find the right tools, design the process, test refine. Whenever I design something new, I treat it like a research project. Define the problem clearly. Choose the most effective tools, run small trials and then adjust based on evidence. There's a line actually from Leonardo da Vinci that I returned to. LA Knowledge is the daughter of experience. That's the bar for my activities. Not just passing an exam, but changing how students approach a problem. Long after the course. AI was just the next tool to test. It doesn't replace the thinking. It frees space for it. It handles the repetitive parts. So I can focus on coaching, pushing and listening In human physiology case studies used to be one size fits all. One case everyone works through the same path. We chat GPT-4 Oh. I built seven different branching medical cases, one per team all running. In parallel. The structure forces each group to make diagnostic decisions, see the consequences and adapt. It's active, it's collaborative, and it's closer to how reasoning works in the field. Students said it gave them a sense of agency one told me it made her seriously consider medical school. Again. After setting that idea aside, at Foothill, I built fse, an owl themed Socratic study partner for anatomy and physiology. Last spring alone, students held over 100 sessions with it, some going 150 messages deep. FTS E doesn't hand over answers. It questions, prompts and checks. Reasoning. The idea is to model expert thinking and then step back allowing students to own that process. For some students, it was actually the first time that they felt confident reasoning through a problem without a person right there. In genetics at Santa Clara University, I redesigned a course around Quest. Question, uncover, explore, solve, and test. So we start with something unresolved data, a pattern, a puzzle. So aida, it's built to make that Struggle Productive, the kind that builds long-term skills instead of frustration. So quick snapshot of how I build anything new. First, I define the learning gap in one sentence, what I am trying to achieve, uh, what I want the students to get from this. Then I prototype with the best tool on hand offering charge PT to draft the prompts or the flow or the rubrics, anything else that I need. Then I pilot with a small group or a single sections, watch what actually happens and then finally iterate. I can tighten the prompts or remove friction or just uh, add enough scaffolding. Uh, so the struggle stays productive. For the branching cases, my workflow is quite simple. I brainstorm differential diagnosis and key decision points with HGBT. Then map the flow in a quick sketch with success and error states. Next, I encode the path in Google forms with conditional logic. I write success or error, micro feedback so students get an immediate signal, not just wrong. And finally, I pressure test it. I try to break by on flow with bad inputs or edge cases until it's stable for seven teams. In parallel, AIDA and FSI are built like research instruments. There's a persona, memory and rules. The persona sets the voice and the scope. The memory tracks the name. Last topic discussed, last misconception, last success by the student and the rules enforce the Socratic pattern. For example, one purposeful question at a time. No answer Provided for work that will be graded and stop. If students ask for shortcuts. I then pilot the prompt log breakdowns, for example, when hints are too cryptic or too generous. And finally I tune the ladder of questions so students stay in that zone where effort turns into insight. After a EA came in, my course evaluations improved on every measure from feedback quality to use of class time concept inventory gains stayed high. Even though I reduced the number of assignments and AI tools for admin work, cut my prep time in half or better so I could put that energy back into design. And mentoring. Students wrote about feeling prepared for the MCAT about learning to reason like physicians, one even said, your class didn't just teach me the material. It changed how I think about my future. That's not the kind of feedback you get from a well polished lecture that comes from practice interaction and iteration. If you are curious about ai, don't overhaul everything at once. Pick one activity and build it out carefully. Keep AI in the co-pilot seat. I'm already looking ahead to G PT five, building full learning apps and scaling assignments so they go beyond fact recall. I want students creating their own apps through vibe coding, essentially designing tools that both deepen their understanding and show what they can do. So two shocks in quick succession. Reshaped how I teach, but in the end, AI didn't subtract anything from my job as a teacher. On the contrary, it gave me more room to be the best teacher I can be. And if we approach the next change with the same mindset, it might be less about disruption and more about what we get to build together with our students. I.
